heroOverview: Hydroureter is abnormal dilation of the ureter — the muscular tube that carries urine from the kidney to the bladder — caused by impaired drainage that allows urine to accumulate and distend the tube. It is not a diagnosis in itself but a radiological sign that marks the position of an obstruction or a source of abnormal backpressure along the urinary tract. The most common causes are urinary stones, ureteral strictures, external tumour compression, and vesicoureteral reflux. Hydroureter is identified on roughly 3-10% of abdominal imaging studies done for any reason, with higher rates in urology practice; its clinical significance ranges from benign transient physiological dilation in pregnancy to a sign of an obstructing cancer that must be investigated urgently. The kidney being damaged first — not the ureter — is the central concern, and the speed and completeness of investigation is calibrated to the degree of dilation and the clinical picture.

keyStats.prevalence: Detected in approximately 3-10% of abdominal ultrasounds and CT scans; found in up to 80% of pregnancies as a physiological finding by the third trimester
keyStats.demographics: Stone-related hydroureter most common in adults aged 20-60; obstructive malignancy more common after age 50; congenital causes present in infancy; physiological pregnancy hydroureter affects the right side preferentially
keyStats.avgAge: Peak diagnostic age varies by cause: stones peak at ages 30-50; pregnancy-related is universal during gestation; malignant obstruction typically presents after age 50
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global incidence not established; kidney stones — a leading cause — affect approximately 1 in 11 people in the United States (NIDDK)

definition: Hydroureter (ICD-10: N13.4) is dilation of the ureter beyond its normal diameter of 3-7 mm, caused by any process that impairs urine flow from the kidney to the bladder. It is defined by aetiology as obstructive (caused by a mechanical or functional blockage — most commonly a stone, stricture, or extrinsic tumour) or non-obstructive (caused by vesicoureteral reflux, high-output urine states, infection, or, most commonly, physiological changes of pregnancy). Obstructive hydroureter may be classified as acute (typically from a stone) or chronic (from a stricture or tumour), and as unilateral (suggesting a local cause) or bilateral (raising concern for pelvic tumour, retroperitoneal fibrosis, or bladder outlet obstruction). The combination of a dilated ureter and a dilated renal pelvis is called hydroureteronephrosis, and it implies that the obstruction has produced back-pressure significant enough to distend the collecting system. Sustained obstructive hydroureter transmits elevated pressure to the glomeruli, impairing filtration and risking permanent renal tubular and glomerular damage if the obstruction is not relieved. Urology is the primary specialty, though gynaecology, oncology, and nephrology are involved depending on cause.

typesClassification.description: Blockage within the ureteral lumen from a stone, blood clot, sloughed papilla, or transitional cell carcinoma; typically causes acute flank pain when from a stone, or painless progressive dilation when from a tumour.

External compression of the ureter by pelvic or abdominal malignancy (cervical, colorectal, lymphoma), enlarged lymph nodes, retroperitoneal fibrosis, or endometriosis; often bilateral when from a midline pelvic mass.

Urine flows retrograde from the bladder into the ureter because the ureterovesical valve is incompetent; the ureter dilates from repeated pressure waves during voiding rather than from outflow obstruction.

Progesterone-induced smooth muscle relaxation plus uterine compression of the right ureter at the pelvic brim produces hydroureter in up to 80% of pregnancies; typically right-sided, asymptomatic, and resolves postpartum.
Primary adynamic ureteral segment that fails to propagate peristalsis; classified and managed as megaloureter when diameter exceeds 7 mm.

primarySymptoms: Acute severe colicky flank or loin pain radiating to the groin or inner thigh — the hallmark of obstructive hydroureter from a ureteral stone, caused by ureteral spasm and capsular distension of the kidney.
Visible haematuria or microscopic blood in the urine, present in approximately 80-90% of stone-related obstructive episodes and also seen with urothelial tumour.
Nausea and vomiting accompanying flank pain, driven by the enteric nervous system's response to ureteral distension.
Dull constant ipsilateral flank or back aching in chronic obstructive hydroureter from a stricture or tumour — often less dramatic than stone colic but persistent.
Urinary frequency, urgency, or dysuria when hydroureter is caused by a low stone near the bladder or when concurrent cystitis is present.
Visible reduction in urine output (oliguria) in bilateral obstructive hydroureter blocking both kidneys, or in a solitary kidney with obstruction.
Fever and rigors when obstructive hydroureter is complicated by pyelonephritis — an emergency requiring immediate decompression.
Asymptomatic incidental finding on imaging: a substantial proportion of hydroureter, particularly from gradual-onset causes, is detected on scans performed for unrelated reasons.
Unintentional weight loss, haematuria, or a palpable pelvic or abdominal mass when hydroureter is caused by urothelial carcinoma or pelvic malignancy.
earlyWarningSigns: A first episode of ipsilateral flank pain after eating or in the evening, which may precede full stone colic as a stone begins its passage.
Mild haematuria on a routine urine dipstick in a patient without symptoms, which should prompt upper-tract imaging to exclude a urothelial lesion or stone.
Progressive unilateral flank discomfort over weeks in a patient known to have pelvic malignancy, suggesting ureteral compression by enlarging disease.
Antenatal ultrasound showing ureteral dilation greater than 7 mm beyond 32 weeks, which may be pathological rather than physiological.
emergencySigns: Fever above 38°C with ipsilateral flank pain in known or suspected hydroureter — infected hydronephrosis/pyonephrosis is a urological emergency requiring immediate decompression with ureteral stenting or nephrostomy and intravenous antibiotics.
Anuria or severe oliguria with bilateral flank pain indicating complete bilateral ureteral obstruction, which causes acute kidney failure within hours.
Signs of urosepsis — confusion, low blood pressure, rapid pulse, and high fever — requiring emergency admission and immediate upper tract decompression.
Severe unremitting pain with vomiting preventing fluid and analgesic intake, requiring hospital admission for intravenous management.

causes.description: A stone passing from the kidney into the ureter lodges most often at three anatomically narrow points — the ureteropelvic junction, the pelvic brim, and the ureterovesical junction — obstructing urine flow and causing acute proximal ureteral dilation; stones smaller than 5 mm pass spontaneously in 90% of cases, while those above 7-8 mm rarely pass without intervention.

Fibrotic narrowing of the ureteral lumen from prior urological instrumentation, radiation therapy, ischaemia, post-infectious scarring (particularly from tuberculosis or schistosomiasis), or following ureteral injury at abdominal surgery; produces progressive chronic hydroureter and hydroureteronephrosis that is often painless until renal function is significantly impaired.

Pelvic tumours — cervical, endometrial, colorectal, ovarian, prostate, and bladder cancers — and retroperitoneal or iliac lymphadenopathy from lymphoma or metastatic disease compress or invade the ureter, causing progressive obstructive hydroureter; bilateral compression producing oliguria is an oncological emergency requiring urgent urological intervention to restore renal function.

Incompetence of the ureterovesical junction allows urine to flow retrograde into the ureter during bladder filling or voiding; high-grade reflux (grades III-V) distends the ureter and collecting system and exposes the kidney to repeated bladder pressure and infected urine, the main mechanism of reflux nephropathy.

Progesterone relaxes ureteral smooth muscle throughout pregnancy; mechanical compression of the right ureter at the pelvic brim by the enlarging uterus and right ovarian vein disproportionately dilates the right side; urine flow is slowed but obstruction is rarely complete, and dilation resolves within 4-6 weeks postpartum.

Fibrous tissue envelops and compresses the retroperitoneal ureters, causing bilateral medial ureteral deviation and obstruction; the idiopathic form is associated with IgG4-related disease, and secondary forms arise from medications (ergot derivatives, beta-blockers), prior aortic surgery, or aortic aneurysm.

Transitional cell carcinoma of the ureteral wall produces an intraluminal obstruction with associated hydroureter upstream; typically presents with painless haematuria; associated with prior bladder urothelial carcinoma in 20-40% of cases and strongly linked to smoking and aristolochic acid exposure.

riskFactors.description: A prior kidney stone confers a 50% five-year recurrence risk; each recurrent stone episode can produce a new hydroureter episode and, if stones impact at the same site repeatedly, cause a stricture.

Cervical, endometrial, colorectal, prostate, ovarian, and bladder cancers collectively account for a large proportion of obstructive hydroureter in adults over 50; any pelvic mass detected on imaging in this age group should prompt ureteral assessment.

Physiological right-sided hydroureter occurs in approximately 80% of pregnancies by the third trimester; it is largely benign but increases susceptibility to pyelonephritis, and pathological obstruction from a stone or tumour can be obscured by physiological dilation.

Radiation to the pelvis for cervical, rectal, or bladder cancer produces delayed ureteral fibrosis and stricture, typically appearing months to years after treatment; late radiation-induced stricture is an important cause of chronic hydroureter in cancer survivors.
High-grade primary VUR detected antenatally or after a first febrile UTI predisposes the ureter to progressive dilation and the kidney to reflux nephropathy.

Enlargement of the prostate causing sustained bladder outlet obstruction raises intravesical pressure, which can propagate retrograde into the ureters over years, producing bilateral secondary hydroureter; prompt treatment of BPH prevents upper tract involvement.

Smoking is the dominant modifiable risk factor for urothelial carcinoma of the ureter and bladder, responsible for approximately 50% of cases; occupational exposure to aromatic amines and a history of bladder cancer also increase risk.

Ureteroscopy, ureteral stent placement, or pelvic surgery — including hysterectomy and bowel resection — can injure the ureteral wall or disrupt blood supply, leading to stricture formation and chronic hydroureter; meticulous surgical technique and post-operative follow-up reduce this risk.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 20-60 with kidney stones (peak incidence of stone colic)
Pregnant women in the second and third trimester (physiological right-sided dilation)
Adults over 50 with pelvic malignancy or urothelial carcinoma
Infants and children with vesicoureteral reflux or congenital ureteral anomalies
Adults with BPH causing bladder outlet obstruction and secondary upper tract dilation
Cancer survivors who received pelvic radiation therapy
Men and women with retroperitoneal fibrosis (IgG4-related disease peak in adults 50-70)
diagnosisOverview: Hydroureter is a radiological finding, so investigation begins with imaging. A low-dose non-contrast CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis (the stone-protocol CT) is the most sensitive and specific first-line test for obstructive hydroureter from a stone, detecting stones as small as 1 mm with sensitivity above 95% and providing simultaneous information on ureteral anatomy and degree of dilation. When radiation avoidance is a priority — in pregnancy, in young patients, or for serial monitoring — renal and bladder ultrasound is the preferred first-line study, though it misses most mid-ureteral stones and calcifications smaller than 3-4 mm. CT urography (with contrast) maps the entire ureter in detail and is the investigation of choice for suspected urothelial tumour, stricture, or extrinsic compression. MAG3 diuretic renogram provides functional data: differential renal function and drainage half-time quantify the degree of obstruction and the residual kidney function on the affected side, which is the key information needed for surgical planning. Retrograde pyelography — performed through a cystoscope placed in the ureteral orifice — provides the most precise anatomical delineation of a stricture before endoscopic or open repair. In all patients with new hydroureter without an obvious benign cause, urine cytology and cystoscopy are performed to exclude urothelial malignancy. Blood tests — creatinine, urea, electrolytes, and CRP — assess renal function and identify sepsis. In bilateral obstruction, urgent decompression takes priority over diagnostic workup.

diagnosticTests.test: Non-contrast CT kidneys, ureters, and bladder (stone protocol CT)
diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line test for suspected stone-related obstruction; identifies the stone, its size and position, and the degree of proximal ureteral and pelvic dilation; also detects extrinsic compression by soft-tissue masses.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Patient lies in the CT scanner for 5-10 minutes; no contrast injection needed; low radiation dose; results available immediately.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Radiation-free first-line imaging for pregnant patients, children, and serial surveillance; reliably detects hydronephrosis and gross ureteral dilation but misses most ureteral stones below the ureteropelvic junction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless gel-based abdominal scan; 20-30 minutes; no preparation needed in most cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detailed assessment of the entire ureter including mucosal defects, strictures, and filling defects from urothelial tumour; the investigation of choice for haematuria with hydroureter in adults over 40.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An iodinated contrast injection is followed by CT scanning in multiple phases; total time 30-60 minutes; requires renal function check before contrast.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures differential renal function (split function between both kidneys) and drainage half-time to quantify the functional significance of the obstruction and guide decisions about decompression or surgery.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Intravenous radiotracer injection followed by furosemide; gamma-camera imaging for 45-60 minutes in lying position; mildly radioactive tracer is excreted in urine within a few hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides the most accurate delineation of stricture length, position, and anatomy, performed via cystoscopy; used for surgical planning before ureteroscopy, balloon dilation, or open ureteral reconstruction.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under general or regional anaesthesia in an operating room setting; a cystoscope is inserted, contrast is injected up the ureter under fluoroscopy; results are immediate.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for urothelial carcinoma of the bladder or ureter; cytology detects shed malignant cells; cystoscopy directly visualises the bladder lining and the ureteral orifices.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Urine cytology requires a morning urine sample; cystoscopy is performed in a procedure room under local anaesthetic gel, taking 10-20 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses current renal function and detects electrolyte disturbances from impaired excretion; essential for all patients with significant hydroureter, especially bilateral disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results within a few hours.

treatmentOverview: Management of hydroureter is directed at the underlying cause and at protecting the affected kidney from sustained pressure-mediated damage. Acute stone-related hydroureter in a patient who is not infected, has adequate pain control, and has a stone smaller than 5-6 mm is managed conservatively: analgesia with NSAIDs and, increasingly, alpha-blocker therapy (tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily) to relax the distal ureteral smooth muscle, which increases the spontaneous passage rate to approximately 75-80% for distal stones under 5 mm within 4 weeks. Larger stones, persistent obstruction, or failure to pass requires urological intervention: ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy achieves stone clearance in over 90% of cases with a low complication rate, while shock wave lithotripsy (SWL) is an alternative for upper ureteral and renal stones within size limits. Emergency ureteral stenting or percutaneous nephrostomy is indicated whenever obstruction is complicated by infection, as decompression must precede definitive stone treatment. Chronic hydroureter from a stricture is treated by endoscopic balloon dilation, incision with a laser or cold knife (endoureterotomy), or open/robotic ureteral reconstruction (ureteroureterostomy, Boari flap, or ileal ureter) depending on stricture length, location, and the degree of renal function impairment. Obstructive hydroureter from extrinsic malignancy that cannot be surgically resected is palliated with a ureteral stent or percutaneous nephrostomy to preserve renal function during oncological treatment. Bilateral obstructive hydroureter is a urological emergency — decompression of one or both sides within hours is required to reverse acute kidney injury.

medicalTreatments.name: Alpha-blocker therapy (tamsulosin)
medicalTreatments.description: Tamsulosin 0.4 mg daily relaxes alpha-1A adrenergic receptors in the distal ureteral smooth muscle, reducing ureteral spasm and facilitating spontaneous stone passage; first-line medical expulsive therapy for distal ureteral stones smaller than 10 mm.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Meta-analyses confirm spontaneous passage rates of 75-80% for distal stones under 5 mm at 4 weeks, versus approximately 55-60% for observation alone; number needed to treat approximately 4-5.

medicalTreatments.name: NSAIDs (ketorolac, diclofenac, ibuprofen)
medicalTreatments.description: The most effective analgesics for renal colic from obstructive stone; prostaglandin synthesis inhibition reduces ureteral spasm, decreases glomerular filtration pressure on the obstructed side, and directly reduces pain intensity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: NSAIDs provide superior analgesia to paracetamol and comparable analgesia to opioids for renal colic with a lower nausea rate, per Cochrane meta-analysis; first-line in patients without renal impairment.

medicalTreatments.name: Ureteral stent (double-J stent)
medicalTreatments.description: A flexible plastic stent is placed between the kidney and bladder through a cystoscope to bypass an obstruction, allowing urine to drain and decompressing the kidney; used acutely for infected obstruction and as a bridge to definitive treatment, and for long-term palliation of inoperable malignant obstruction.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves upper tract decompression in approximately 95% of cases; stent-related symptoms (frequency, dysuria, urgency, flank pain) affect 70-80% of stented patients and require management.

medicalTreatments.name: Percutaneous nephrostomy
medicalTreatments.description: Image-guided placement of a tube through the back directly into the renal collecting system to drain urine externally; used when cystoscopic stenting fails, in antegrade approaches to stone treatment, and in selected malignant obstructions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Successful decompression in approximately 95% of cases with acute obstructive nephropathy; allows kidney function to recover in the majority when obstruction is relieved within days to weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Glucocorticoids for IgG4-related retroperitoneal fibrosis
medicalTreatments.description: High-dose prednisolone (40-60 mg daily) is first-line for IgG4-related retroperitoneal fibrosis causing bilateral hydroureter; produces radiological response and ureteral liberation in the majority of patients, often avoiding surgical ureterolysis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Objective radiological response in approximately 70-80% of IgG4-related retroperitoneal fibrosis, with ureteral decompression typically evident within 4-8 weeks of starting treatment.

surgicalOptions.name: Ureteroscopy with laser lithotripsy
surgicalOptions.description: A flexible or semi-rigid ureteroscope is advanced through the urethra and bladder into the ureter; a holmium laser fragments the obstructing stone into pieces small enough to pass spontaneously or be extracted with a basket under direct vision. The procedure is performed under general anaesthesia, usually as day surgery.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Stone clearance rates of 90-95% for ureteral stones with a single procedure; complication rate below 5% in experienced centres; brief ureteral stent for 1-2 weeks typically placed post-procedure.

surgicalOptions.name: Shock wave lithotripsy (SWL)
surgicalOptions.description: Focused high-energy shock waves delivered externally fragment stones in the upper ureter and kidney; suitable for stones smaller than 10 mm in the proximal ureter with no bleeding disorder or uncorrected coagulopathy. Does not require anaesthesia in most cases.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Stone-free rates of approximately 70-80% for proximal ureteral stones at 3 months; lower success for impacted, hard stones (e.g. calcium oxalate monohydrate); may require repeat sessions.

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic balloon dilation or laser endoureterotomy
surgicalOptions.description: Minimally invasive endoscopic treatment for short ureteral strictures (less than 2 cm); a balloon catheter dilates the scarred segment or a laser incises it under direct ureteroscopic vision, followed by prolonged ureteral stenting for 4-6 weeks while the ureter heals.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Success rates of 55-75% for benign short strictures; lower for longer or post-radiation strictures; appropriate for patients unfit for open surgery.

surgicalOptions.name: Open or robotic ureteral reconstruction (ureteroureterostomy / Boari flap / ileal ureter)
surgicalOptions.description: For long strictures, radiation-induced damage, or failed endoscopic repair: the diseased ureteral segment is excised and the ureter is reconstructed by end-to-end anastomosis (ureteroureterostomy for mid-ureteral strictures), a bladder flap (Boari flap for distal strictures), or a bowel segment (ileal ureter for extensive upper ureteral damage). Robotic assistance improves precision and reduces recovery time.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Success rates of 80-90% for open and robotic ureteroureterostomy; Boari flap and ileal ureter have longer-term success rates of approximately 85% in published series; these are major reconstructive procedures carrying risks of anastomotic leak, bowel complications, and prolonged recovery.

alternativeTreatments.name: Increased oral fluid intake for stone-related hydroureter
alternativeTreatments.description: Drinking 2-3 litres of fluid daily maintains urine dilution, supports spontaneous stone passage, and reduces stone recurrence; the most evidence-based and universally applicable preventive measure for stone disease.

alternativeTreatments.name: Observation and serial imaging for physiological pregnancy hydroureter
alternativeTreatments.description: Physiological right-sided hydroureter of pregnancy requires only periodic renal function monitoring and vigilance for infection; ureteral stenting or nephrostomy is reserved for the minority with proven obstruction, stone, or pyelonephritis.

whySeeSpecialist: All patients with new hydroureter should be referred to a urologist unless a clearly self-limiting cause — physiological pregnancy dilation or a small distal stone on medical expulsive therapy — has been confirmed. Urothelial malignancy must be excluded in all adults over 40 with haematuria and a dilated ureter, and any hydroureter from extrinsic compression by a pelvic mass requires urgent oncological and urological assessment. Bilateral hydroureter with rising creatinine is a urological emergency — same-day specialist input is essential.
doctorSelectionGuide: For stone-related hydroureter, seek a urologist with endoscopic training in flexible ureteroscopy and laser lithotripsy, particularly for stones not expected to pass spontaneously. For complex strictures or ureteral reconstruction, look for a centre offering robotic reconstructive urology. For malignant obstruction, a urological oncologist working within a multidisciplinary cancer team provides the most integrated approach. In pregnancy, a urologist experienced in managing stones in gravid patients — who can defer radiation and co-manage with obstetrics — provides optimal safety.
preventionStrategies: Maintain fluid intake of at least 2-3 litres daily to keep urine dilute and prevent stone formation — the single most effective preventive measure for stone-related hydroureter.
Avoid prolonged use of non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs and other nephrotoxic agents that can damage the ureteral mucosa and predispose to stricture.

Ensure post-operative ureteral assessment when abdominal or pelvic surgery involving proximity to the ureter is performed — intra-operative ureteral stenting is used prophylactically in high-risk cases.
Treat vesicoureteral reflux in children with antibiotic prophylaxis and, where indicated, surgical correction to prevent progressive ureteral dilation and renal scarring.
Monitor renal function with annual creatinine and ultrasound in patients known to have partial chronic hydroureter from a stricture or extrinsic compression, to detect silent deterioration.
lifestyleModifications: Drink at least 2-3 litres of fluid daily, spread throughout the day, to prevent stone recurrence — the most important lifestyle change for patients with stone-related hydroureter.
Reduce dietary sodium to below 2,300 mg/day and animal protein to recommended levels for stone formers, as both increase urinary calcium and oxalate excretion.
Maintain a healthy BMI — obesity increases urinary excretion of stone-forming compounds and is associated with a higher stone recurrence risk.
Attend all follow-up imaging appointments to detect recurrence of obstruction or renal deterioration before symptoms develop.

In patients with malignant or radiation-related ureteral obstruction, watch for stent blockage symptoms (returning flank pain, fever, reduced urine output) and follow the stent exchange schedule precisely.

recommended: At least 2-3 litres of water or low-sugar fluid daily to maintain urine output above 2 litres and dilute stone-forming solutes
Adequate dietary calcium (1,000-1,200 mg/day from food, not supplements) to bind intestinal oxalate and reduce urinary oxalate excretion in calcium oxalate stone formers
A diet rich in fruits and vegetables, which increases urinary citrate (a natural stone inhibitor) and alkali load
Low dietary sodium to reduce urinary calcium excretion
avoid: High-oxalate foods such as spinach, rhubarb, and nuts in large quantities for calcium oxalate stone formers
Excess animal protein, which increases urinary uric acid, calcium, and oxalate and reduces urinary pH and citrate
High-dose vitamin C supplements exceeding 1,000 mg daily, which are metabolised to oxalate and increase stone risk

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise supports a healthy weight and normal urinary flow and is encouraged in all patients with hydroureter. During an acute stone episode with severe colic, rest and analgesics are appropriate; gentle activity can resume once pain is controlled. Vigorous high-impact exercise during ureteral stenting may worsen stent-related symptoms; a urologist should advise based on stent position and comfort level. After ureteral reconstruction, return to full activity typically takes 4-8 weeks.
dailyManagement: Measure and record daily fluid intake to ensure consistent hydration goals are met — a target urine output of over 2 litres per day is measurable with a collection jug if stone prevention is the priority.
If a ureteral stent is in place, monitor for fever, severe flank pain, reduced urine output, or frank bleeding, and contact the urology team promptly if these develop.
Take tamsulosin at the same time each evening and maintain adequate fluid intake during medical expulsive therapy for a stone.
Seek same-day medical review for any fever while managing known hydroureter — infected obstruction evolves rapidly to sepsis.
Attend stent exchange appointments without delay; stents are typically changed every 3-6 months in palliative settings and every 6-12 weeks in post-operative settings to prevent encrustation.
prognosis: The outlook for hydroureter depends entirely on its underlying cause and the speed with which obstruction is relieved. Stone-related acute hydroureter has an excellent prognosis: 80-90% of stones smaller than 7 mm pass without intervention, and ureteroscopy clears the rest with a high success rate and minimal long-term renal impairment if treatment is not delayed beyond days to weeks. Physiological pregnancy hydroureter resolves spontaneously postpartum without renal consequences in the vast majority. Chronic hydroureter from a benign stricture treated before renal function falls below 40% can be fully reconstructed with preservation of function. The prognosis is more guarded when obstruction from malignancy is only palliable rather than curable — ureteral stenting maintains quality of life and renal function during chemotherapy or radiation, but recurrent stent dysfunction and blockage require ongoing urological management. Prolonged untreated bilateral obstructive hydroureter causing severe renal impairment may not fully recover even after decompression, particularly when obstruction has been present for more than a few weeks.
recoveryTimeline: Stone-related hydroureter with successful medical expulsive therapy: stone passage and symptom resolution within 2-4 weeks in the majority. Post-ureteroscopy: ureteral stent discomfort for 5-14 days; most patients return to work within 1-7 days. Post-open or robotic ureteral reconstruction: hospital stay 3-7 days; return to light activity at 2-4 weeks, full activity at 6-8 weeks; imaging confirmation of successful anastomosis at 6-12 weeks. Physiological pregnancy hydroureter: resolution within 4-6 weeks of delivery.
complications: Obstructive nephropathy — sustained elevated pressure damages renal tubules and glomeruli; even partial resolution leaves scar tissue that cumulatively reduces filtration capacity, particularly with recurrent obstruction.
Pyelonephritis and urosepsis — infected urine behind an obstruction can rapidly evolve to septic shock; fatality risk is significant without early decompression and antibiotics.
Ureteral stricture formation as a late consequence of stone passage, ureteroscopy, or radiation, causing chronic hydroureter requiring reconstructive surgery.

Post-obstructive diuresis — after relief of bilateral or complete unilateral obstruction, a brisk salt-and-water diuresis can cause dehydration and electrolyte disturbances if not monitored and replaced.

Stent-related morbidity — indwelling ureteral stents cause frequency, dysuria, urgency, and flank pain in up to 80% of patients; encrustation and occlusion occur if stents are not exchanged on schedule.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Kidney Stones information
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient-facing resource on kidney and ureteral stones from the U.S. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases.

supportResources.name: NHS — Kidney stones
supportResources.description: Plain-English NHS guide to stone-related ureteral obstruction, treatment options, and prevention.

supportResources.name: Urology Care Foundation — Ureteral Obstruction
supportResources.description: Patient information on ureteral obstructions, causes, and treatment from the American Urological Association's patient education arm.

sources.title: Seitz C et al. Medical therapy to facilitate urinary stone passage. Lancet, 2006 — alpha-blockers for expulsive therapy
Pearle MS et al. Medical management of kidney stones: AUA guideline (2014, amended 2019)
Nakashima MN et al. Ureteral stenting and nephrostomy: review of complications. International Journal of Urology, 2021
Jhanwar A et al. Ureteral stricture: current management. Turkish Journal of Urology, 2020
NICE NG118 — Renal and ureteric stones: assessment and management (2019)

confusedWithConditions.name: Megaloureter
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Megaloureter specifically means a ureter exceeding 7 mm in diameter, usually implying a congenital structural abnormality. Hydroureter is the broader descriptive term for any ureteral dilation of any size and any cause, including transient stone obstruction or pregnancy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Nephrolithiasis (kidney stones in the renal pelvis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Nephrolithiasis refers to stones within the kidney; when a stone moves into the ureter it becomes ureterolithiasis and causes hydroureter. Hydroureter is the consequence of the stone's position, not the stone itself.

confusedWithConditions.name: Vesicoureteral reflux
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: VUR causes hydroureter from retrograde backflow of urine rather than from downstream obstruction; both dilate the ureter but management differs entirely — reflux is evaluated by VCUG rather than stone-protocol CT.

relatedConditions.name: Kidney stones
relatedConditions.relevance: Urinary stones are the most common acute cause of hydroureter; every stone lodging in the ureter produces upstream dilation until the stone passes or is removed.

relatedConditions.name: Megaloureter
relatedConditions.relevance: Megaloureter is a specific subtype of hydroureter defined by diameter exceeding 7 mm, usually from a congenital adynamic segment; the two terms overlap and are often used interchangeably in clinical radiology reports.

relatedConditions.name: Benign prostatic hyperplasia
relatedConditions.relevance: Longstanding BPH raises intravesical pressure and can produce bilateral secondary hydroureter; assessing the upper tract is part of BPH workup when symptoms are severe.

relatedConditions.name: Acute pyelonephritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Pyelonephritis arising behind an obstructed, hydroureter-producing stone or stricture is an emergency requiring immediate decompression; the two conditions frequently coexist and each worsens the other.

relatedConditions.name: Renal cell carcinoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Renal tumours extending into the collecting system or compressing the renal pelvis can produce ipsilateral hydroureter; new hydroureter with haematuria in adults should exclude renal malignancy.

relatedConditions.name: Vesicoureteral reflux
relatedConditions.relevance: High-grade VUR is a major cause of non-obstructive hydroureter in children; its management pathway — antibiotic prophylaxis and sometimes ureteral reimplantation — overlaps substantially with congenital megaloureter.

faqs.question: What is hydroureter?
faqs.answer: Hydroureter means the ureter — the tube carrying urine from the kidney to the bladder — is abnormally widened because urine cannot drain freely. It is a radiological finding that points to an obstruction or abnormal backpressure somewhere along the urinary tract.

faqs.question: What causes hydroureter?
faqs.answer: The most common causes are kidney stones lodged in the ureter, ureteral strictures from scarring, external tumour compression, and vesicoureteral reflux. In pregnancy, the expanding uterus and progesterone naturally dilate the right ureter in up to 80% of cases.

faqs.question: Is hydroureter serious?
faqs.answer: It depends entirely on the cause. A small stone causing temporary dilation is common and usually resolves within weeks. A slowly growing cancer compressing the ureter can cause silent kidney damage. Any new hydroureter without an obvious benign cause should be investigated promptly.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hydroureter and hydronephrosis?
faqs.answer: Hydroureter means the ureter is dilated. Hydronephrosis means the kidney's collecting system is dilated. When both are present together — called hydroureteronephrosis — it means the obstruction is severe or longstanding enough to back up into the kidney as well as the ureter.

faqs.question: How is hydroureter diagnosed?
faqs.answer: A non-contrast CT scan is the most accurate first test for stone-related hydroureter. Ultrasound is used in pregnancy and for children. CT urography maps the entire ureter with contrast for strictures or tumours. A MAG3 renogram measures how much obstruction is affecting kidney function.

faqs.question: Can hydroureter go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Yes, when the cause resolves. Most small ureteral stones pass within 2-4 weeks, and the hydroureter resolves with them. Physiological pregnancy hydroureter resolves within 4-6 weeks of delivery. Hydroureter from a stricture or tumour will not resolve without treatment.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for hydroureter from a kidney stone?
faqs.answer: Stones smaller than 5 mm are treated with pain relief (NSAIDs) and tamsulosin to relax the ureter and help the stone pass, with a success rate of about 75-80%. Larger stones or those causing infection need ureteroscopy with laser fragmentation, which clears stones in over 90% of cases.

faqs.question: What is a ureteral stent and when is it needed for hydroureter?
faqs.answer: A ureteral stent is a thin plastic tube placed from the kidney to the bladder through a cystoscope to bypass an obstruction and drain urine. It is used as emergency decompression when an obstructed ureter becomes infected, and for longer-term palliation of obstruction from cancer or inoperable stricture.

faqs.question: Can hydroureter damage the kidney permanently?
faqs.answer: Prolonged or severe obstruction transmits backpressure to the kidney and can cause irreversible tubular and glomerular damage. The risk is greatest with bilateral obstruction, obstruction lasting weeks, or repeated episodes. Early relief of obstruction preserves kidney function.

faqs.question: Is hydroureter common in pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Yes. Physiological right-sided hydroureter occurs in up to 80% of pregnancies by the third trimester, caused by progesterone relaxation of the ureteral muscle and mechanical uterine compression. It is almost always benign and resolves after delivery.

faqs.question: Can hydroureter cause a urinary tract infection?
faqs.answer: Yes. Stagnant urine in a dilated ureter is a breeding ground for bacteria, and infection — including acute pyelonephritis — is one of the most common and dangerous complications of obstructive hydroureter. Fever with flank pain in a patient with known hydroureter is a urological emergency.

faqs.question: What is bilateral hydroureter?
faqs.answer: Bilateral hydroureter means both ureters are dilated simultaneously. It suggests a cause affecting both sides, such as a pelvic tumour compressing both ureters, bladder outlet obstruction from an enlarged prostate, or retroperitoneal fibrosis. Bilateral obstruction can rapidly cause acute kidney failure.

faqs.question: Does hydroureter need surgery?
faqs.answer: Not always. Small stones causing temporary hydroureter often pass without surgery. Pregnancy-related hydroureter needs no intervention. Larger stones, strictures, and tumour compression usually do need urological procedures — ranging from minimally invasive ureteroscopy to open ureteral reconstruction — depending on severity.

faqs.question: What symptoms does hydroureter cause?
faqs.answer: Stone-related hydroureter causes severe colicky flank pain radiating to the groin with nausea and sometimes blood in the urine. Chronic causes from a stricture or tumour produce a dull constant ache or no pain at all. Bilateral obstruction may cause reduced urine output.

faqs.question: Can an enlarged prostate cause hydroureter?
faqs.answer: Yes. Longstanding bladder outlet obstruction from benign prostatic hyperplasia raises intravesical pressure, which can propagate retrograde into both ureters over years, producing bilateral secondary hydroureter. This is one reason urologists assess the upper urinary tract in men with severe or longstanding BPH symptoms.

faqs.question: How do I know if my hydroureter is obstructive or not?
faqs.answer: A MAG3 diuretic renogram measures how well the ureter drains and how much the obstruction has affected kidney function. A drainage half-time above 20 minutes after furosemide injection indicates obstruction. CT and ultrasound show the anatomy but cannot reliably distinguish obstructive from non-obstructive dilation.

faqs.question: What causes a ureteral stricture leading to hydroureter?
faqs.answer: Ureteral strictures form from scarring after a stone injury, urological instrumentation, pelvic radiation therapy, tuberculosis, or ureteral injury during abdominal surgery. The scar narrows the ureteral lumen and chronically backs up urine, causing progressive hydroureter and kidney damage.

faqs.question: Is hydroureter visible on ultrasound?
faqs.answer: Ultrasound reliably detects gross hydroureter and hydronephrosis but misses most ureteral stones below the ureteropelvic junction. It is the preferred first-line test in pregnancy and children due to the absence of radiation, but CT is more sensitive for the full assessment of obstructive causes in adults.

faqs.question: Can hydroureter be prevented?
faqs.answer: Stone-related hydroureter can largely be prevented with consistent high fluid intake (2-3 litres daily) and dietary adjustments for stone formers. Vesicoureteral reflux-related hydroureter in children benefits from antibiotic prophylaxis and sometimes corrective surgery. Malignant causes are harder to prevent but are diagnosable early with surveillance imaging.

faqs.question: What is post-obstructive diuresis?
faqs.answer: After an obstructed kidney is decompressed, it may produce a large volume of urine rapidly — post-obstructive diuresis. In bilateral obstruction this can cause dehydration and salt loss and requires monitoring and fluid replacement in hospital, particularly in the first 24-48 hours after stenting or nephrostomy.

regionalNames.name: Hydroureteronephrosis
regionalNames.region: Clinical medicine (when kidney is also dilated)

metaDescription: Hydroureter is a dilated ureter caused by stones, strictures, or tumours. Learn symptoms, imaging tests, and treatments from ureteral stents to surgery.
